text: We present a case of laparoscopic total gastrectomy, omentectomy, splenectomy, or gastric adenocarcinoma. A seventy-six year old male presents with past medical history of pernicious anemia, hypertension, transient ischemic attack, hypothyroidism, dyslipidemia, pulmonary embolism, and prostate cancer. His past surgical history included open suprapubic subtotal prostatectomy done eight years ago and a bilateral inguinal hernia repair, both done at an outside hospital. He underwent screening upper gastrointestinal endoscopy for anemia at our institution, which showed a small size lesion in the fundus of stomach. Biopsies obtained from the lesion came diagnostic for gastric adenocarcinoma.
The preoperative imaging reveals a small mass located in the fundus of the stomach, confirming the presence of gastric adenocarcinoma.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy to assess for hepatic, peritoneal, and distant metastasis. The dissection of the greater omentum begins on the left side of the transverse colon and is completed on the right side.

The surgeon performs dissection along the gastroepiploic blood vessels and station IV lymph nodes, followed by dissection in the retroduodenal space and skeletonization of station V and VI lymph nodes in the suprapyloric and intrapyloric areas. The duodenum is transected distal to the pylorus, and the duodenal stump is oversewn with two OO absorbable sutures. Dissection continues along the pars flaccida and station III lymph nodes along the lesser curvature, where the left gastric vessels are identified, dissected off station VII lymph nodes, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon dissects the retrogastric space, skeletonizing station eleven lymph nodes. Dissection continues along the right and left crura, skeletonizing station one and two lymph nodes. The phrenoesophageal fat pad is dissected, and the esophagus is mobilized into the abdominal cavity, followed by its transection. Dissection progresses along the greater curvature of the stomach towards the spleen, detaching posterior and lateral splenic attachments. The splenic hilum is identified, and the splenectomy is completed with specimen retrieval.

The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y operation, creating a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy between the biliopancreatic limb and the common channel. The Roux limb is advanced into the left upper quadrant, and an esophago-jejunostomy is completed using a 25 mm circular stapler. The candy cane is transected, and anchoring sutures are placed on the left and right crura to ensure a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test to assess the patency of the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis, ensuring there are no leaks at the surgical site.

The specimens collected were total gastrectomy, omentectomy and splenectomy and there were no intraoperative complications. The surgical pathology of the specimens retrieved showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with dysplasia in background of chronic atrophic gastritis with multifocal intestinal metaplasia. Metastatic adenocarcinoma was seen in four of the twenty-eight lymph nodes retrieved. Omentum showed no evidence of tumor. Spleen was histologically undemarkable and the splenic artery showed focal arterial calcification at splenic hilum. Two splenic hilar lymph nodes came negative for cancer.
This was T-II, N-I, M-Zero cancer and stage II according to AJCC classification.

The patient was transitioned to a fluid diet by post-operative day II. He received an upper GI series which came back normal on post-operative day III. The drains were removed and the drain amylase came negative on the post-operative day five. The patient was discharged home in a stable condition on a post-operative day six with adequate pain control and the bowel movements present.
He was started on a Folfox therapy for twelve cycles on post-operative week seven. Here's an image showing the normal upper GI series. on the post-operative day three. Thank you.
